items.question_text: Under 14-2-1, for which types of voice communication requirements is CPDLC approved to augment?
items.explanation: 14-2-1(a) lists seven categories: altitudes, routes, speeds, holding clearances, altimeters, advisories, and frequency changes. CPDLC augments — but does not replace — voice for these items.

choices.text: All altitudes, routes, speeds, holding clearances, altimeters, advisories, and frequency changes.
Only altitudes, routes, and speed assignments; holding and advisory messages remain voice-only.
All voice communication requirements, including immediate and expeditious clearances.
Only altimeter settings and frequency changes; routes and speeds require voice.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-1, who is responsible for sending and responding to CPDLC messages?
items.explanation: 14-2-1(b) assigns responsibility to the sector team for both sending and responding to CPDLC messages.

choices.text: sector team

items.question_text: Under 14-2-1, what must the last controller working an aircraft before it exits the continental U.S. ensure regarding CPDLC?
items.explanation: 14-2-1(g): unless otherwise coordinated, the last controller must ensure the CPDLC connection is terminated upon transfer to any non-U.S. facility or ATOP sector.

choices.text: The CPDLC connection is transferred seamlessly to the non-U.S. facility.
A new CPDLC connection is pre-established with the receiving oceanic sector.
The CPDLC connection is terminated upon transfer of communication to any non-U.S. facility or ATOP sector.
The pilot is instructed to terminate the CPDLC connection upon entering oceanic airspace.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-1, what coordination is required before terminating a CPDLC connection from a position other than the sector with eligibility?
items.explanation: 14-2-1(h): coordination must be accomplished with the sector with eligibility prior to terminating a CPDLC connection from any other position or adapted air traffic workstation.

choices.text: No coordination is needed if the termination is from a supervisor position.
Coordination must be accomplished with the sector with eligibility prior to termination.
Coordination is only needed with the receiving sector, not the sector with eligibility.
The termination may proceed immediately if an abnormal indication is present in the FDB.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-1, what does the automated Voice Communication Indicator (VCI) comply with during CPDLC operations?
items.explanation: 14-2-1(f): use of the automated VCI during CPDLC operations complies with the requirements of 2-1-17, Radio Communications. The VCI is treated as meeting voice communication rules.

choices.text: The requirements of FAA Order JO 7110.65 paragraph 2-1-17, Radio Communications.
The requirements of JO 7210.3, Facility Operation and Administration, for data link communications.
The requirements of Chapter 14 only, with no cross-reference to other paragraphs.
The requirements of ICAO Document 4444 for CPDLC voice indicator standards.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-1, controllers should _____ the use of CPDLC during critical phases of flight.
items.explanation: 14-2-1(c) states controllers should minimize the use of CPDLC during critical phases of flight. This is a should, not a must, but the operational intent is clear.

choices.text: minimize

items.question_text: Under 14-2-2, when must abnormal CPDLC indications be acknowledged by the controller?
items.explanation: 14-2-2(b): abnormal CPDLC indications must be acknowledged by the controller only after required coordination has been performed. The acknowledgment follows coordination, not precedes it.

choices.text: Immediately upon appearing in the FDB and ACL, to clear the indication for operational continuity.
Only after required coordination has been performed.
Only after the pilot acknowledges the abnormal indication via voice.
At the controller's discretion, with no specific timing requirement.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-2, what is the prescribed phraseology for overriding an open CPDLC clearance by voice?
items.explanation: 14-2-2(c) provides exact phraseology: DISREGARD CPDLC (type) CLEARANCE (description of clearance) AND SEND AN UNABLE (alternate clearance). The pilot must send an UNABLE to close the uplink.

choices.text: CANCEL CPDLC (type) CLEARANCE AND ACKNOWLEDGE (alternate clearance).
DISREGARD CPDLC (type) CLEARANCE (description of clearance) AND SEND AN UNABLE (alternate clearance).
OVERRIDE CPDLC (type) CLEARANCE, NEW CLEARANCE (alternate clearance).
DISREGARD CPDLC MESSAGE AND COMPLY WITH (alternate clearance).

items.question_text: Under 14-2-2, why must the controller tell the pilot to send an UNABLE when overriding a CPDLC clearance?
items.explanation: 14-2-2(d) note 2: canceling an uplink only removes it from the ground system; the uplink remains open on the flight deck. The pilot must respond with UNABLE to close the uplink on the flight deck. Additionally, the note in 14-2-2(c) warns the clearance may not have been received on the flight deck yet.

choices.text: Canceling an uplink only removes it from the ground system; the pilot must send UNABLE to close it on the flight deck.
UNABLE is required only for FANS 1/A aircraft that cannot process ROGER responses.
The UNABLE response is a formality; the system closes the uplink automatically when the controller cancels it.
It is optional; the pilot may send WILCO or ROGER instead, whichever is more appropriate.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-2, for NORDO aircraft with an active CPDLC connection, what is permissible for the sector with eligibility?
items.explanation: 14-2-2(e)(1): for NORDO aircraft with an active CPDLC connection, it is permissible for the sector with eligibility to mark the aircraft on frequency to allow CPDLC communications with that aircraft.

choices.text: Mark the aircraft on frequency to allow CPDLC communications with that aircraft.
Terminate the CPDLC connection and rely on light gun signals for all further communication.
Transfer the CPDLC connection to the nearest ATOP sector for oceanic handling.
Maintain voice-only communication and disable CPDLC for the affected aircraft.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-3, when a CPDLC connection is unexpectedly lost and voice communication had not previously been established, what must the controller do?
items.explanation: 14-2-3(b): the controller must ensure voice communication is established and maintained with that aircraft. The key condition is that voice was not previously established — if it was, voice already exists as a backup.

choices.text: Ensure voice communication is established and maintained with that aircraft.
Wait for the aircraft to re-establish the CPDLC connection automatically.
Send a CPDLC free-text message requesting the aircraft to re-establish connection.
Transfer the aircraft to the next sector and let them establish voice communication.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-3, automated altimeters are only sent in response to which two specific events?
items.explanation: The note to 14-2-3(a) states automated altimeters are only sent in response to a monitor transfer of communication (TOC) or an altitude uplink when the assigned altitude is below FL180.

choices.text: A monitor transfer of communication (TOC) and an initial contact message from the aircraft.
A monitor transfer of communication (TOC), or an altitude uplink when the assigned altitude is below FL 180.
An altitude uplink when above FL180 and a pilot-initiated downlink requesting the setting.
Every frequency change and every transfer of communication, regardless of altitude.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-3, what must controllers do regarding open or abnormally closed uplinks at the time of a CPDLC shutdown?
items.explanation: 14-2-3(c)(2): controllers must take action to resolve any open or abnormally closed uplinks, by voice, with each aircraft at the time of shutdown.

choices.text: by voice

items.question_text: Under 14-2-4, what reference must controllers comply with when using abbreviations in weather-related or advisory/free text CPDLC messages?
items.explanation: 14-2-4(a)(2): when using abbreviations to compose weather-related or advisory/free text messages, controllers must comply with FAA Order JO 7340.2, Contractions.

choices.text: FAA Order JO 7110.65, Chapter 2, Section 1, General Control.
FAA Order JO 7340.2, Contractions.
FAA Order JO 7210.3, Facility Operation and Administration.
ICAO Document 4444, PANS-ATM, for all CPDLC abbreviations.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-4, CPDLC must not be used to issue a speed adjustment to an aircraft established on a route or procedure that has _____.
items.explanation: 14-2-4(b)(2): CPDLC must not be used to issue a speed adjustment to an aircraft established on a route or procedure that has published speed restrictions.

choices.text: published speed restrictions

items.question_text: Under 14-2-4, why must an aircraft be cleared out of holding via voice rather than CPDLC?
items.explanation: The note to 14-2-4(c)(1): a route uplink does not specify a new clearance limit, so clearing an aircraft out of holding must be done via voice. The CPDLC system cannot convey the new clearance limit in a route uplink message.

choices.text: A route uplink does not specify a new clearance limit, so the clearance out of holding cannot be fully conveyed by CPDLC.
CPDLC holding messages have an NE response attribute that prevents the pilot from acknowledging the exit.
The WILCO response attribute for holding messages cannot be upgraded to support clearance-limit changes.
Holding is always considered a critical phase of flight, and CPDLC must be minimized during critical phases.

items.question_text: Under 14-2-4, what must be done before CPDLC can be used to issue holding instructions to an aircraft with a climb/descend via clearance?
items.explanation: 14-2-4(c)(2) and its note: if an aircraft has a climb/descend via clearance, holding instructions must not be issued via CPDLC. The vertical navigation portion must be canceled prior to using CPDLC for holding.

choices.text: The climb/descend via clearance itself prevents CPDLC holding; only voice can be used for holding instructions.
The vertical navigation portion of the procedure must be canceled prior to using CPDLC to issue holding instructions.
The holding instructions must be combined with a route uplink that specifies the new clearance limit.
The aircraft must be below FL180 before CPDLC can issue holding while a climb/descend via is active.

items.situation: An aircraft is approaching the boundary where it will exit the continental U.S. and transfer to an ATOP oceanic sector. The aircraft has an active CPDLC connection with your sector. A trainee wants to leave the CPDLC connection active through the transfer because the aircraft has an open trajectory-altering clearance that has not been fully resolved.
items.question_text: How should you handle the CPDLC connection and the open clearance before transfer?

choices.text: Ensure there are no open trajectory-altering clearances before transfer (unless otherwise coordinated), and terminate the CPDLC connection upon transfer to the ATOP sector.
Leave the CPDLC connection active so the ATOP sector can use it, and transfer the open trajectory-altering clearance with the handoff.
Terminate the CPDLC connection from any position without coordination, since the transfer to ATOP requires it.
Keep both the CPDLC connection and the open TAC active; the ATOP sector's system will handle both automatically.

items.explanation: 14-2-1(E) requires no open TACs before transfer unless otherwise coordinated. 14-2-1(g) requires CPDLC termination upon transfer to non-U.S. facility or ATOP. 14-2-1(h) requires coordination with the eligibility sector before terminating from another position.

items.question_text: Which of the following best summarizes the rule for CPDLC use during emergency pilot-initiated downlinks?

choices.text: Use CPDLC free text to respond to the emergency, following the message elements in TBL 14-2-21.
Follow the provisions of FAA Order JO 7110.65, Chapter 10, Emergencies.
Terminate the CPDLC connection immediately and revert all communication to voice without exception.
Issue an UNABLE response via CPDLC and instruct the pilot to contact the next sector.

items.explanation: 14-2-1(I): in the event of receipt of an emergency PID, follow Chapter 10, Emergencies. The CPDLC-specific handling defers to the broader emergency procedures.

items.situation: You need to override an open CPDLC route clearance that contained three elements: direct Memphis, cross 30 west of Nashville at FL310, and maintain 280 knots. A trainee wants to cancel the uplink from the ground system first, then issue only the changed speed element by voice, and acknowledge the abnormal FDB indication without coordinating with the sector that has eligibility.
items.question_text: How should you properly override this multi-element CPDLC clearance?

choices.text: Use voice to override with the full DISREGARD phraseology, restate the entire clearance, instruct the pilot to send UNABLE, complete required coordination before acknowledging any abnormal indication, and cancel the uplink only after the pilot acknowledges the superseding voice clearance.
Cancel the CPDLC uplink first to clear the indication, then issue only the changed element by voice.
Acknowledge the abnormal indication immediately to clear the FDB, then override the clearance element-by-element using CPDLC.
Issue the new speed by voice without referencing the CPDLC clearance, since the pilot will see the uplink cancellation automatically.

items.explanation: 14-2-2(C): use voice, restate entire multi-element clearance. 14-2-2(b): acknowledge abnormal indications only after required coordination. 14-2-2(d): cancel uplink only after pilot acknowledges superseding voice clearance. The pilot must send UNABLE.

items.question_text: What is the correct voice broadcast when CPDLC service shuts down or fails?

choices.text: "Attention all aircraft; CPDLC service interrupted. Avoid downlinking until CPDLC is restored."
"All stations, CPDLC failure. Use voice for all communications until further notice."
"Attention all aircraft; CPDLC no longer in use. Do not downlink any messages until further advised."
"CPDLC shutdown in effect. Pilots should monitor voice frequency for further instructions."

items.explanation: 14-2-3(C)(1) contains the exact phraseology: 'Attention all aircraft; CPDLC no longer in use. Do not downlink any messages until further advised.'

items.situation: An aircraft is holding at a published fix with a climb via clearance still active in the system. The aircraft needs holding instructions revised with a new altitude. A trainee wants to send the revised holding instructions via CPDLC because the holding message elements in TBL 14-2-2 support altitude assignment within holding clearances.
items.question_text: Can the revised holding instructions be issued via CPDLC?

choices.text: Yes, because CPDLC holding messages in TBL 14-2-2 can include altitude assignment.
No, because the vertical navigation portion of the climb via procedure must be canceled before CPDLC can be used for holding instructions.
Yes, because holding with altitude assignment via CPDLC is permitted when the aircraft is already in the hold.
No, because holding instructions can never be issued via CPDLC under any circumstances.

items.explanation: 14-2-4(C)(2): if an aircraft has a climb/descend via clearance, holding instructions must not be issued via CPDLC. The note says the vertical nav portion must be canceled prior to using CPDLC for holding.
